items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: What causes the supply curve to shift?
items.correct: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes/subsidies, number of producers, expectations — anything affecting supply other than the good's own price.
items.misconception: When demand rises, the supply curve shifts right to meet it.
items.random_wrong: Supply shifts only when consumers want more.
items.why: Rising demand causes a movement along the supply curve, not a shift of it. Supply shifts only when something on the supply side changes. Students entangle the two curves.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: Which is most likely to RAISE UK productivity in the long run?
items.correct: Investing in worker skills and new tech
items.why: Correct answer: Investing in worker skills and new tech. “Cutting training budgets” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Falling unit costs as output expands is called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Economies of scale. “Inflation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Workforce 50, output rises from 1,000 to 1,500 units in same hours. % productivity rise?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
